subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — President vs. Congress in Domestic Policy Making
stimulus: Over time, there has been conflict between the president and Congress over their roles in domestic policy making. Develop an argument as to whether the president or Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 70, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position on president vs. Congress in domestic policy. Generic statements about the conflict existing do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The president should have more power because presidents are more effective leaders.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Presidents should have more power over domestic policy making and they are better equipped for the role.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Congress should have more power because its decisions reflect the will of the majority.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Congress should have more power over domestic policy making since members are the directly elected representatives of the people.”

wrong_examples: “The president should have more power over domestic policy making.” Do not respond to the prompt
“Congress was stronger than the presidency in the nineteenth century.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR

required_elements: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Federalist No. 51 was written by James Madison.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 51 was written by James Madison.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “The Articles of Confederation were adopted before the
alternatives.correct_example: “The Articles of Confederation were adopted before the

required_elements: “The president has the power to appoint cabinet officials who oversee the bureaucracy.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The president has the power to appoint cabinet officials who oversee the bureaucracy.”

required_elements: “The Congress has the power to determine the national budget.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The Congress has the power to determine the national budget.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 51 discusses the need for a government that checks the ambition of individuals.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 51 discusses the need for a government that checks the ambition of individuals.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 70 discusses the role of the executive.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 70 discusses the role of the executive.”

required_elements: “Article I lists the enumerated powers of Congress.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I lists the enumerated powers of Congress.”

required_elements: “Presidents and Vice-Presidents are the only elected officials that represent all Americans.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Presidents and Vice-Presidents are the only elected officials that represent all Americans.”

required_elements: “Through iron triangles, Congress can collaborate with the bureaucracy and interest groups.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Through iron triangles, Congress can collaborate with the bureaucracy and interest groups.”

required_elements: “In Federalist No. 51, Madison writes that one branch cannot become too powerful.”
alternatives.correct_example: “In Federalist No. 51, Madison writes that one branch cannot become too powerful.”

required_elements: “In Federalist No. 70, Hamilton writes that only a unified executive brings energy and safety to the nation.”
alternatives.correct_example: “In Federalist No. 70, Hamilton writes that only a unified executive brings energy and safety to the nation.”

required_elements: “Article I of the Constitution gives Congress significant power over domestic policy making, including the power to pass laws and to approve the budget.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Article I of the Constitution gives Congress significant power over domestic policy making, including the power to pass laws and to approve the budget.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the relationship between evidence and the argument.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Informal powers give the president the ability to make policies quickly, so the president should have more power over domestic policy making.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The founders were concerned that, without a strong presidency, the national government would not be able to lead the nation during times of domestic crisis and disorder. This is why they wanted a strong executive.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Senate rules that require a supermajority ensure that the policymaking process produces some level of consensus, so Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Congress is the closest to the citizens, and a strong executive will interfere with the ability of Congress to establish policies that meet the needs of citizens.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Representation is important, and while members of Congress represent districts and states, only the president represents all Americans.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some people might argue that Congress is better for domestic policy making because it better represents the people, but better representation could result in Congressional gridlock and slow down the policy making process.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “The president might make policy quicker, but Congress’s slower process is actually better because it allows for more voices to be heard.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some might argue it is better for the president to lead on domestic policy making because the president has direct access to the resources of the federal government, this ignores the fact that without proper checks, the presidency can use those resources to threaten liberty and states’ rights.”

wrong_examples: “Presidents should not have more power when it comes to domestic policy making.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not rebut or refute that perspective
“On the other hand, some people might argue that presidents should have more power in domestic policymaking because they can make quick decisions.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
